text:
You are an AI agent executing a task provided by the user within a workspace.

## Workspace layout

Everything you work with lives under one workspace root. **`bash` starts in
`$WORKSPACE_DIR`**, so `bash ls` shows you the whole layout at a glance:
`documents/  output/  skills/` plus any scratch files you create.

- **`$WORKSPACE_DIR`** — your working area, default `bash` cwd. Use it for
  notes, intermediate files, and skill output. Skill scripts live at
  `$WORKSPACE_DIR/skills/<name>/scripts/`.
- **`$DOCUMENTS_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/documents`) — task documents.
  Read-only.
- **`$OUTPUT_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/output`) — deliverables. The harness
  routes relative `write` and `edit` paths here automatically.
- **Task configuration** (`task.json`) — contains the task definition and the
  grading rubric. Do not read, search, or reference it. Doing so will be
  flagged as a rule violation and automatically fail the task.

## Tool conventions

- Use `read` to consume input files (handles .docx, .xlsx, .pptx, .pdf, and
  plain text).
- Use the file-type skill manuals below to produce binary deliverables
  (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx).
- Use `write` only for plain markdown — typically a `response.md`
  summarizing your work.
- Use `edit` for incremental refinement of a file you have already created.

## Context management (automatic)

To keep your context small and your runs cheap, the harness automatically
elides the *raw text* of large `read`/`bash` outputs after they have been on
screen for a few turns, replacing each with a short `[context-saver: …]` stub
that names the file or command. The document was fully read when it was
current — nothing was hidden from your initial pass — only the bulk is dropped
from later turns. Two habits keep this lossless:

- As you read a document, **record the specific facts, figures, dates, and
  verbatim clauses you will need** into your notes or draft. Don't rely on the
  full text staying resident.
- If you later need the exact text of a doc that now shows a `[context-saver:
  …]` stub, simply **`read` that file again** — the full content returns.

## File-format skills

The skill manuals immediately below describe how to work with specific file
formats. Read them before tackling the task.


## Skill: docx

---
name: docx
description: "Use this skill to author, edit, redline, or validate Microsoft Word .docx files. Covers creating new documents from markdown or templates, editing existing documents in place, generating tracked-changes redlines, adding comments, and accepting/rejecting revisions. For READING existing .docx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'draft a memo', 'mark up the agreement', 'redline this', 'add comments to', 'fill the engagement letter template'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .xlsx, .pptx, or .doc (legacy Word)."
---

# DOCX authoring, editing, redlining

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .docx, use the harness `read` tool. It already returns structured text via pandoc. This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *validating*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Generate a new doc from markdown | `scripts/generate_from_md.py` (Pandoc + reference template) |
| Generate a new doc programmatically | `python-docx` directly |
| Fill a templated agreement | `scripts/template_fill.py` (docxtpl / Jinja) |
| Edit an existing doc | `scripts/unpack.py` → mutate XML → `scripts/pack.py` |
| Produce a tracked-changes redline | `scripts/redline.py` |
| Add comments to a passage | `scripts/comments_add.py` |
| Accept all redlines | `scripts/accept_changes.py` |
| Validate a docx before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` (mandatory final step) |

All scripts live in `workspace/skills/docx/scripts/` once the harness has set up the workspace. Invoke them via `bash`.

## Creating a new document

Pick by what you have:

- **Markdown content + a styled firm template** → `generate_from_md.py input.md out.docx template.docx`. Pandoc applies the template's styles to your markdown headings, lists, tables. Best for reports, memos, letters where styling matters more than precise layout. Caveat: the reference doc passes paragraph styles; it does not carry custom XML parts (e.g., comment threads).
- **A template with named placeholders** → `template_fill.py template.docx context.json out.docx`. docxtpl renders Jinja2 expressions inside the template. Best for engagement letters, NDAs, structured agreements.
- **Programmatic build** → write Python using `python-docx`. Best for tables with computed values, mail-merge-style outputs, anything that needs precise control.

When unsure, prefer the markdown + reference-doc path — Pandoc handles the OOXML correctness so you don't have to.

## Editing an existing document

Three-step pattern:

```bash
python scripts/unpack.py input.docx workdir/
# edit XML files under workdir/word/
python scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.docx
python scripts/validate.py output.docx
```

Key files inside the unpacked tree:

- `word/document.xml` — body content (paragraphs, runs, tables)
- `word/styles.xml` — paragraph + run style definitions
- `word/numbering.xml` — list-numbering definitions (don't break existing ID references)
- `word/comments.xml` — comment thread content (created by `comments_add.py`)
- `word/header*.xml`, `word/footer*.xml` — running headers/footers
- `[Content_Types].xml` — MIME registration for every part. Edit when you add a new part type.
- `_rels/` and `word/_rels/` — relationships between parts. Edit when you reference a new image, comment, or external resource.

### Run-merging gotcha

Word writes adjacent runs (`<w:r>`) with identical formatting separately. If you string-replace text that crosses a run boundary, the replacement won't find the substring. `unpack.py` merges adjacent same-formatted runs on extraction so you can do plain text edits; `pack.py` is permissive about whatever run structure you write back.

### Smart-quote escaping

Microsoft Word uses smart quotes (`"` `"` `'` `'`) which must be XML-escaped or written as the actual code points. `unpack.py` substitutes them with XML entities for editing safety; `pack.py` reverses the substitution before zipping. Don't manually re-escape — let the scripts handle it.

### Whitespace preservation

Trailing whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements is significant. Both `unpack.py` and `pack.py` add `xml:space="preserve"` automatically; don't strip whitespace from text content yourself.

## Redlines (tracked changes)

```bash
python scripts/redline.py original.docx revised.docx redlined.docx \
  --author "Reviewer" --date "2026-04-30"
```

Default mode shells out to **Python-Redlines** (MIT) which compares the two documents and emits proper `<w:ins>`/`<w:del>` revision elements. Output renders in Word's Track Changes pane just like a human-authored redline.

### Manual mode

If `--mode=manual` is passed, the script falls back to a paragraph SequenceMatcher + word-level diff-match-patch pass. Use this when:
- Python-Redlines fails on a particular doc structure (rare).
- You want to control which paragraphs are diffed.
- The change is purely formatting (`<w:rPrChange>` runs) rather than text.

### What gets tracked

- Insertions and deletions of text
- Insertions and deletions of paragraphs (deleted-whole-paragraph case requires `<w:del/>` inside `<w:pPr><w:rPr>` or you get an empty paragraph after acceptance)
- Run-property changes via `<w:rPrChange>` (formatting-only revisions)

### What doesn't get tracked

- Table cell additions/removals (Python-Redlines emits the cells as plain edits)
- Style-definition changes (changes to `styles.xml` aren't revision-trackable)
- Image swaps

## Comments

```bash
python scripts/comments_add.py document.docx comments.json
```

`comments.json` is a list of `{anchor_text, author, comment}` objects. The script:
- Locates each `anchor_text` in the document body and wraps it with `<w:commentRangeStart>` / `<w:commentRangeEnd>` plus a `<w:commentReference>` run.
- Creates or appends to `word/comments.xml` (with proper id assignment).
- Patches `[Content_Types].xml` and `word/_rels/document.xml.rels` if commenting is being added for the first time.

Anchor matching is exact-string. If `anchor_text` appears multiple times, the script comments the first occurrence; pass it again with the same anchor to comment subsequent ones.

`<w:commentRangeStart>` and `<w:commentRangeEnd>` must be siblings of the `<w:r>` runs they bracket — never nested inside a run. If you write comments by hand, follow this rule.

## Accept / reject changes

```bash
python scripts/accept_changes.py redlined.docx accepted.docx
```

Uses LibreOffice headless via a documented StarBasic macro:

```basic
Sub AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.store() End Sub
```

LibreOffice must be installed and on PATH (`soffice` binary). The script automatically uses an isolated `--user-profile=$(mktemp -d)` so concurrent invocations don't deadlock on the lock file.

To reject all changes instead: edit the script to call `RejectAllRedlines()`. To accept selectively: this isn't supported — open the doc in Word.

## Validation gate

**Always run `validate.py` before declaring the task complete.**

```bash
python scripts/validate.py output.docx
```

Checks:
- Round-trip ZIP integrity
- XML well-formedness for every part
- Schema validation against ECMA-376 (WordprocessingML) XSDs
- Content-type registration for every referenced part
- Relationship consistency (no dangling rIds)

Exit code 0 = valid. Non-zero exit code with line-number diagnostics = fix and re-pack.

## Common pitfalls

- **Legacy `.doc` (binary) is not supported.** Convert with `soffice --convert-to docx input.doc` first.
- **List numbering breaks after edits.** Numbering lives in `word/numbering.xml` keyed by `numId`. If you delete a list, also delete its numId reference; if you reorder, don't change IDs.
- **Headers and footers are separate parts** (`word/header1.xml`, etc.). Edits to body content don't touch them.
- **Pandoc reference-doc passes paragraph styles only.** Custom XML parts (comments, tracked changes baseline) are not carried over.
- **Don't pretty-print whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements.** Pretty-printing breaks runs that depend on exact spacing.
- **Tables: dual width specs.** Each cell needs both `columnWidths` (in `<w:tblGrid>`) and per-cell `<w:tcW w:type="dxa">`. Percentages (`pct`) render fine in Word but break in Google Docs.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- Producing PDFs from .docx: pipe through `soffice --convert-to pdf` after this skill is done.
- Signing / encryption / DRM.
- Word macros (`.docm` with VBA).



## Skill: pptx

---
name: pptx
description: "Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft PowerPoint .pptx files. Covers generating decks from scratch (HTML+PptxGenJS, Marp markdown-to-slides, or python-pptx), editing existing decks in place, and validating output. For READING existing .pptx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a deck', 'create slides', 'edit slide N', 'add a chart slide'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .xlsx, or .ppt (legacy)."
---

# PPTX authoring and editing

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .pptx, use the harness `read` tool (markitdown extracts slide text). This skill is for *writing* and *editing*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Generate a deck from scratch (HTML/CSS) | `scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js` |
| Generate a deck from markdown | `scripts/generate_marp.sh` |
| Build slides programmatically | `python-pptx` directly |
| Edit a shape on an existing slide | `scripts/edit_shape.py` (JSON patch) |
| Add or remove a slide | unpack → edit → pack |
| QA a deck deterministically | `scripts/deterministic_qa.py` |
| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |

## Generation modalities

**HTML/CSS via PptxGenJS** (preferred for visual fidelity):
```bash
node scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js deck.json out.pptx
```
`deck.json` describes slides as a JSON tree; the script invokes PptxGenJS (and html2pptx for HTML inputs) to produce a fully-editable .pptx. Best for branded decks with gradients, custom fonts, complex shapes.

**Markdown via Marp**:
```bash
bash scripts/generate_marp.sh deck.md out.pptx
```
Best for content-heavy decks (lectures, reports) where markdown is more natural than JSON.

**Programmatic via python-pptx**:
Best when shapes are computed (e.g., one slide per data row). Requires manual EMU positioning.

## Editing existing decks

Three-step pattern, like docx:
```bash
python scripts/unpack.py input.pptx workdir/
# edit XML files under workdir/ppt/slides/
python scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.pptx
python scripts/validate.py output.pptx
```

For surgical shape edits without unpacking, use `edit_shape.py`:
```bash
python scripts/edit_shape.py input.pptx \
  --slide 2 --shape "Title 1" --op set_text --value "New title"
```

JSON patch ops: `set_text`, `set_position` (EMU), `set_size`, `recolor`, `delete`.

## OOXML gotchas specific to pptx

- **Use `defusedxml.minidom`, NOT `xml.etree.ElementTree`.** ElementTree corrupts presentation namespaces during round-tripping. `unpack.py` and `pack.py` use minidom; if you write your own XML manipulation, do the same.
- **EMU units everywhere.** 1 inch = 914400 EMU. Slide positions, sizes, font sizes (in pt × 100) all use derived EMU values.
- **Placeholders vs free shapes.** Placeholders inherit from slide masters; free shapes don't. Editing a placeholder's text is `<a:t>` content; editing its layout requires master-slide changes.
- **Don't pretty-print pptx XML on pack.** Whitespace-significant runs (`<a:r>`) break if reformatted. `pack.py` preserves the original whitespace.
- **Slide cloning is more than a file copy.** Use `unpack` + `pack` — manual file copies miss the rIds in `_rels/` and the `Content_Types.xml` registration.

## Deterministic QA loop

After every generation, run:

```bash
python scripts/thumbnail.py deck.pptx thumbs/   # PDF + JPEGs per slide
python scripts/deterministic_qa.py deck.pptx > qa.json
```

`deterministic_qa.py` checks:
- Shape bounding boxes don't extend past slide edges
- No two shapes overlap with > 50% area intersection
- Font sizes ≥ 11pt for body text, ≥ 18pt for titles
- All placeholders are filled (no `Click to add title` defaults)
- Bullet lists don't exceed 7 items per slide

Output is JSON listing each violation with slide number and shape id. Fix violations and re-render.

(A vision-model QA pass is intentionally out of scope for v1. Add `--use-vision` later if deterministic checks miss layout issues.)

## Validation gate

**Always run `validate.py` before declaring done.** Schema-validates against ECMA-376 PresentationML XSDs, checks rId consistency, content-type registration.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- SmartArt creation (limited python-pptx support).
- Complex embedded charts beyond what python-pptx exposes.
- Slide transitions / animations (rarely matter for legal output).
- Vision-model layout review (deferred to v2).



## Skill: xlsx

---
name: xlsx
description: "Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft Excel .xlsx files. Covers building workbooks with formulas, editing existing files, recalculating formulas, and scanning for #REF!/#DIV/0!/#VALUE! errors. For READING existing .xlsx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a model', 'create a spreadsheet', 'fill the schedule', 'recalculate'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .pptx, or .xls (legacy Excel)."
---

# XLSX authoring and editing

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .xlsx, use the harness `read` tool (pandas extracts every sheet as a markdown table). This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *recalculating*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Build a workbook from scratch | `openpyxl` directly, or `scripts/build_workbook.py` for banker conventions |
| Edit cells in an existing file | `openpyxl.load_workbook(...)` → mutate → save |
| Recalculate formulas (full fidelity) | `scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py` |
| Recalculate formulas (no LibreOffice) | `scripts/recalc_pure_python.py` |
| Scan for formula errors | `scripts/scan_errors.py` |
| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |

## Banker conventions (mandatory for financial models)

Apply these to every workbook unless the task explicitly overrides:

- **Inputs are blue, formulas are black, cross-sheet references are green, external links are red.** Use `Font(color='0000FF')` etc.
- **Negatives in parentheses, not minus signs.** Use number format `#,##0;(#,##0)`.
- **Red negatives in P&L tables.** Use `#,##0;[Red](#,##0)`.
- **Accounting format for currency.** `_-* #,##0_-;-* #,##0_-;_-* "-"_-;_-@_-` (or the localized equivalent).
- **Multiples shown as `0.0x`**, not `0.0` followed by an "x" character. Format: `0.0"x"`.
- **Underline-only on totals**, not bold-and-underline. Use `Border(bottom=Side(style='thin'))`.
- **No merged cells in input ranges.** Merged cells break formulas that reference them; reserve merging for headers and titles only.
- **Units in adjacent cells**, not in the cell with the value. `($M)` next to the value, not `"$1,234M"` as a string.

`scripts/build_workbook.py` applies these conventions automatically given a JSON spec.

## Formula authoring

- **Always emit formulas, never calculated values.** If the user wants `revenue × growth`, write `=B2*C2`, not `1234.56`. The recalc step materializes values.
- **Use named ranges** for cross-sheet inputs. `wb.defined_names["assumptions"] = DefinedName(...)`. Easier to audit.
- **Document units in adjacent cells** so the model is self-explanatory.
- **No volatile functions in hot paths.** `OFFSET`, `INDIRECT`, `NOW`, `TODAY` recalculate on every change and slow large workbooks.

## Recalculation — choose your engine

`openpyxl` writes formula *strings*; it does not evaluate them. You must recalculate before delivery, otherwise consumers will see `=B2*C2` literal text where they expect numbers (in some readers) or stale cached values (in others).

**LibreOffice path** (`recalc_libreoffice.py`) — ground truth:
```bash
python scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py input.xlsx output.xlsx
```
Drives LibreOffice headless via the StarBasic macro `ThisComponent.calculateAll(); ThisComponent.store()`. Slow (~5–10s per workbook) but matches Excel for nearly every function. Use this when the workbook contains modern Excel features.

**Pure-Python path** (`recalc_pure_python.py`) — fast, partial:
```bash
python scripts/recalc_pure_python.py input.xlsx output.xlsx
```
Uses `xlcalculator` to evaluate every formula in pure Python. Fast (~0.5s per workbook). Covers ~80% of common functions: arithmetic, `SUM`, `IF`, `VLOOKUP`, `INDEX`/`MATCH`, basic string/date functions.

**Does NOT support**: `XLOOKUP`, `LET`, dynamic arrays (`FILTER`, `SEQUENCE`, `UNIQUE`), `LAMBDA`, `BYROW`, `TEXTJOIN` with refs, structured table references, most modern (post-2019) Excel features.

If you used any of those, run the LibreOffice path. The pure-Python path is for CI environments without LibreOffice.

## Error scan

After every recalc, scan for formula errors:

```bash
python scripts/scan_errors.py output.xlsx > errors.json
```

Reports every cell whose computed value matches `#REF!`, `#DIV/0!`, `#VALUE!`, `#NAME?`, `#NULL!`, `#NUM!`, or `#N/A`. Output is JSONL with `{sheet, address, value}` per line.

If errors exist, fix and re-recalc. Don't ship a workbook with `#REF!`s — it's the most common reason a deliverable fails QA.

## Validation gate

`scripts/validate.py output.xlsx` schema-validates against ECMA-376 SpreadsheetML XSDs and confirms ZIP integrity, content-type registration, sheet relationships.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- **PivotTables** — `openpyxl` round-trips existing pivots but cannot create or modify them. If a task requires pivot creation, escalate (Windows-only via COM, not portable).
- **DAX measures and Power Pivot** — `openpyxl` can't write these. Same limitation.
- **VBA macros (`.xlsm`)** — out of scope for this skill. Macros require Excel runtime.
- **Conditional formatting beyond simple cell-value rules** — `openpyxl` supports basic CF; complex rules (top-N, data bars across sheets) often fail to round-trip.
- **Charts beyond the python-pptx-equivalent set** — line/bar/scatter work; combo charts and trendlines round-trip unreliably.

result:
**[CITY OF EVANSTON, ILLINOIS]{.underline}**

**[MUNICIPAL CODE]{.underline}**

**Title 9 --- Licenses, Permits, and Business Regulations**

**Article 5 --- Cannabis Business Establishments**

Adopted by the City Council of the City of Evanston, Cook County, Illinois, Ordinance No. 78-O-23, effective January 1, 2024, as amended. This Article is adopted pursuant to the authority granted to the City of Evanston under the Illinois Cannabis Regulation and Tax Act, 410 ILCS 705/, and in the exercise of the City\'s home rule powers under Article VII, Section 6 of the Illinois Constitution of 1970. Codified at Title 9, Chapter 9, Article 5 of the Evanston City Code.

Right-click to update Table of Contents

**[Section 9-5-1. Title and Purpose]{.underline}**

**(A)** This Article shall be known and may be cited as the \"City of Evanston Cannabis Business Establishments Ordinance.\"

**(B)** The purpose of this Article is to establish comprehensive local regulations governing the permitting, licensing, operation, and oversight of cannabis business establishments within the corporate limits of the City of Evanston, consistent with and supplementary to the Illinois Cannabis Regulation and Tax Act (410 ILCS 705/) and the implementing rules and regulations adopted thereunder by the Illinois Department of Financial and Professional Regulation, the Illinois Department of Agriculture, and other applicable State agencies.

**(C)** The specific goals and objectives of this Article are as follows:

> \(1\) To protect the public health, safety, and general welfare of the residents and visitors of the City of Evanston;
>
> \(2\) To prevent the sale, distribution, or dispensing of cannabis and cannabis-infused products to persons under twenty-one (21) years of age;
>
> \(3\) To promote social equity and ensure that individuals and communities disproportionately impacted by cannabis prohibition and enforcement have meaningful opportunities to participate in the legal cannabis industry within the City;
>
> \(4\) To minimize adverse impacts of cannabis business establishments on surrounding residential neighborhoods, commercial districts, schools, daycare facilities, and other sensitive land uses;
>
> \(5\) To ensure the orderly and responsible integration of cannabis business establishments into the commercial fabric of the City of Evanston, in a manner that complements existing land uses and contributes positively to the economic vitality of the community;
>
> \(6\) To establish clear and transparent standards for the application, evaluation, issuance, renewal, and revocation of local cannabis business establishment permits;
>
> \(7\) To promote environmental sustainability in the operation of cannabis business establishments; and
>
> \(8\) To generate revenue for the City of Evanston that supports community reinvestment, restorative justice programs, workforce development, and public health initiatives, particularly in communities designated as Restorative, Reinvest, and Renew (R3) zones.

**(D)** This Article shall be liberally construed to effectuate the foregoing purposes. In the event of any ambiguity, the provisions of this Article shall be interpreted in favor of protecting public health and safety.

**[Section 9-5-2. Definitions]{.underline}**

For the purposes of this Article, the following terms shall have the meanings ascribed to them in this Section. Terms not defined herein shall have the meanings assigned to them in the Illinois Cannabis Regulation and Tax Act (410 ILCS 705/) or, if not defined therein, their common and ordinary meaning.

**(A) \"Agent\"** means any officer, director, manager, employee, or volunteer of a cannabis business establishment who is twenty-one (21) years of age or older and has been issued a valid Illinois Cannabis Dispensing Organization Agent Identification Card by the Illinois Department of Financial and Professional Regulation. The term \"Agent\" includes both full-time and part-time employees and any person who performs services for or on behalf of the cannabis business establishment in a capacity that involves the handling, sale, or dispensing of cannabis or cannabis-infused products.

**(B) \"Agent-in-Charge\"** means the individual designated by a cannabis dispensary as the on-site manager responsible for day-to-day operations and compliance with this Article and all applicable State law during all hours in which the dispensary is open for business. The Agent-in-Charge shall be a licensed agent who has been authorized by the dispensary\'s principal officers to act on behalf of the establishment in matters of regulatory compliance, security, and customer service.

**(C) \"Applicant\"** means any person, corporation, limited liability company, partnership, or other business entity that has filed or intends to file an application for a cannabis business establishment permit under this Article. The term \"Applicant\" includes all principal officers and any person who holds a financial or voting interest of five percent (5%) or greater in the applying entity.

**(D) \"Calendar Quarter\"** means the standard calendar quarters of each year, defined as follows: Quarter 1 (January 1 through March 31), Quarter 2 (April 1 through June 30), Quarter 3 (July 1 through September 30), and Quarter 4 (October 1 through December 31). All references in this Article to quarterly obligations, reports, or deadlines shall be calculated based on these fixed calendar quarter periods.

**(E) \"Cannabis Business Establishment\"** means a cultivation center, craft grower, infuser organization, dispensing organization, processing organization, or transporting organization, as those terms are defined in the Illinois Cannabis Regulation and Tax Act (410 ILCS 705/), or any successor legislation. The term \"Cannabis Business Establishment\" encompasses both adult-use and medical cannabis operations authorized under State law.

**(F) \"Cannabis Dispensary\"** or **\"Dispensing Organization\"** means a facility operated by a person or entity that is licensed by the State of Illinois under the Illinois Cannabis Regulation and Tax Act to acquire cannabis from licensed cannabis business establishments for the purpose of selling or dispensing cannabis, cannabis-infused products, cannabis seeds, paraphernalia, or related supplies to purchasers, qualifying patients, and designated caregivers. The term includes both adult-use dispensing organizations and medical cannabis dispensing organizations.

**(G) \"Cannabis Licensing Coordinator\"** means the City official designated by the Director of the Department of Community Development to administer this Article, process applications for cannabis business establishment permits, conduct compliance reviews, and coordinate enforcement activities. The Cannabis Licensing Coordinator may delegate specific administrative functions to qualified staff within the Department of Community Development.

**(H) \"Community Benefit Contribution\"** means the annual monetary contribution required of all cannabis dispensary permit holders, as set forth in Section 9-5-15 of this Article, payable to the City of Evanston Cannabis Community Benefit Fund.

**(I) \"Community Engagement Session\"** means a publicly noticed meeting held by an applicant within the corporate limits of the City of Evanston for the purpose of informing local residents, business owners, and community stakeholders about the proposed cannabis business establishment and soliciting public input regarding the proposed use.

**(J) \"Daycare Center\"** means any facility licensed by the State of Illinois to provide child care services, including but not limited to day care centers, day care homes, and group day care homes as defined in the Child Care Act of 1969 (225 ILCS 10/), or any successor legislation.

**(K) \"K-12 School\"** means any public or private institution providing elementary or secondary education to students in kindergarten through twelfth grade, including charter schools, magnet schools, and parochial or other religiously affiliated schools.

**(L) \"Permit\"** means a local cannabis business establishment permit issued by the City of Evanston under this Article authorizing the operation of a cannabis business establishment at a specified location within the City.

**(M) \"Principal Officer\"** means any person who has a financial or voting interest of five percent (5%) or greater in a cannabis business establishment, or who participates in the management or direction of the establishment, including but not limited to owners, members, partners, officers, directors, and managers. The term includes both direct and indirect ownership interests.

**(N) \"R3 Area\"** or **\"R3 Zone\"** means a geographic area within the City of Evanston designated by the City Council as a Restorative, Reinvest, and Renew zone, identified by reference to U.S. Census Bureau census tracts as set forth in Section 9-5-19(G) of this Article, for purposes of the City\'s Social Equity Program.

**(O) \"Retail Floor Area\"** means the portion of a cannabis dispensary\'s total square footage that is accessible to customers for the purpose of viewing, selecting, and purchasing cannabis products. Retail Floor Area does not include secure storage areas, office or employee-only areas, restrooms, vestibules, waiting areas, hallways used exclusively for employee access, loading docks, or mechanical rooms. The Retail Floor Area shall be calculated based on the interior dimensions of the customer-accessible sales space as depicted on the applicant\'s approved floor plan.

**(P) \"Social Equity Applicant\"** means an applicant that meets one or more of the eligibility criteria set forth in Section 9-5-19 of this Article.

**(Q) \"State License\"** means a license issued by the State of Illinois, through the Illinois Department of Financial and Professional Regulation, the Illinois Department of Agriculture, or any other authorized State agency, authorizing the operation of a cannabis business establishment under the Illinois Cannabis Regulation and Tax Act (410 ILCS 705/).

**[Section 9-5-3. Cannabis Business Establishment Permit Required]{.underline}**

**(A)** No person, corporation, limited liability company, partnership, or other entity shall operate, maintain, manage, conduct, or otherwise carry on the business of a cannabis business establishment at any location within the corporate limits of the City of Evanston without first obtaining:

> \(1\) A valid State license issued by the appropriate State of Illinois licensing authority authorizing the specific type of cannabis business operation proposed; and
>
> \(2\) A local cannabis business establishment permit issued under this Article for the specific location at which the cannabis business establishment is to be operated.

**(B)** The possession of a State license shall not, by itself, entitle any person or entity to operate a cannabis business establishment within the City of Evanston. A local permit issued under this Article is a separate and independent requirement.

**(C)** A local cannabis business establishment permit issued under this Article does not authorize any activity that is not also authorized by a valid State license. The permit holder must at all times operate within the scope of both the local permit and the State license.

**(D)** Operating a cannabis business establishment within the City of Evanston without a valid local permit issued under this Article shall constitute a violation of the Evanston City Code and shall be subject to the fines, penalties, and enforcement actions set forth in Section 9-5-25 of this Article, in addition to any other remedies available to the City under applicable law.

**[Section 9-5-4. Number of Permits; Types of Cannabis Business Establishments]{.underline}**

**(A)** The City Council shall, by resolution, establish the maximum number of cannabis business establishment permits available in each category of cannabis business establishment. The City Council may increase or decrease the maximum number of permits by subsequent resolution, taking into consideration the public interest, market conditions, community impact, and the goals and objectives of this Article.

**(B)** As of the effective date of this Article, the City of Evanston authorizes a maximum of four (4) cannabis dispensary permits for adult-use and/or medical dispensing organizations operating within the corporate limits of the City. This maximum may be adjusted by the City Council by resolution at any time.

**(C)** As of the effective date of this Article, no permits are authorized for cultivation centers, craft growers, infuser organizations, processing organizations, or transporting organizations. The City Council reserves the right to authorize permits for such cannabis business establishment types by future resolution, subject to such terms and conditions as the City Council may deem appropriate.

**(D)** Cannabis business establishment permits issued under this Article are non-transferable, except as expressly provided in Section 9-5-24 of this Article. Any attempted transfer in violation of Section 9-5-24 shall be void and shall constitute grounds for immediate permit revocation.

**(E)** A permit issued under this Article is specific to the location identified in the application and may not be used to authorize operations at any other location without a new application and approval process as set forth in this Article.

**[Section 9-5-5. Application Requirements]{.underline}**

**(A)** Any person or entity seeking a cannabis business establishment permit under this Article shall submit a complete application to the Cannabis Licensing Coordinator at the Department of Community Development, Lorraine H. Morton Civic Center, 2100 Ridge Avenue, Evanston, Illinois 60201, during an open application window as designated by the City pursuant to Section 9-5-7 of this Article.

**(B)** Each application shall include the following materials, documents, and information:

> \(1\) A completed application form in the format prescribed by the Cannabis Licensing Coordinator, containing all information required therein;
>
> \(2\) Proof of a valid State of Illinois conditional or permanent cannabis dispensing organization license issued by the Illinois Department of Financial and Professional Regulation, or documentation demonstrating that the applicant has a pending application for such license in good standing with the State;
>
> \(3\) Copies of the applicant\'s articles of organization or articles of incorporation, operating agreement or corporate bylaws, and a current certificate of good standing issued by the Illinois Secretary of State within the preceding sixty (60) days;
>
> \(4\) A complete list of all principal officers of the applicant, including each principal officer\'s full legal name, date of birth, current home address, ownership percentage or voting interest, role or title within the organization, and Social Security number. This list shall be updated to reflect any changes within five (5) business days of such change;
>
> \(5\) Proof of the applicant\'s legal right to occupy the proposed premises, which shall consist of either an executed lease agreement with a remaining term of not less than three (3) years from the anticipated date of permit issuance, or a recorded deed demonstrating the applicant\'s fee simple ownership of the premises;
>
> \(6\) A detailed site plan and floor plan of the proposed premises, prepared by a licensed architect or engineer, including the total square footage of the premises and a breakdown of retail floor area, secure storage area, office and employee-only space, restrooms, vestibule and waiting area, and any other functional areas. The floor plan shall clearly delineate all areas accessible to customers and all areas restricted to authorized personnel;
>
> \(7\) Evidence of compliance with the buffer zone and distance requirements set forth in Section 9-5-9 of this Article, including a scaled map or survey prepared by a licensed land surveyor or professional engineer registered in the State of Illinois, showing the distances from the proposed cannabis dispensary premises to the nearest K-12 school, daycare center, and any other licensed cannabis dispensary within the City of Evanston;
>
> \(8\) A comprehensive business plan, including a market analysis, financial projections for a minimum of three (3) years, a detailed staffing model including the anticipated number of full-time and part-time employees, a management structure and organizational chart, and a description of the applicant\'s operational plan for the proposed dispensary;
>
> \(9\) A security plan meeting all requirements of Section 9-5-13 of this Article, including specifications for video surveillance, alarm systems, access controls, inventory tracking, cash handling procedures, and emergency response protocols;
>
> \(10\) An odor mitigation plan, including HVAC system specifications, carbon filtration system details, air exchange rates, and a certification by a licensed professional engineer registered in the State of Illinois that the proposed odor mitigation system is adequate to prevent the migration of cannabis-related odors beyond the property line of the premises;
>
> \(11\) An environmental sustainability plan addressing energy efficiency measures, waste reduction strategies, water conservation, and cannabis waste disposal procedures consistent with the regulations of the Illinois Environmental Protection Agency and the Illinois Cannabis Regulation and Tax Act;
>
> \(12\) A community benefits plan meeting the requirements of Section 9-5-15 of this Article;
>
> \(13\) A diversity and inclusion plan meeting the requirements of Section 9-5-20 of this Article;
>
> \(14\) Documentation of community engagement sessions conducted in compliance with Section 9-5-8 of this Article, including all materials specified therein;
>
> \(15\) Evidence of insurance coverage meeting all requirements of Section 9-5-16 of this Article, or a binder, commitment letter, or letter of intent from a qualified insurance carrier demonstrating the applicant\'s ability to obtain such coverage prior to permit issuance;
>
> \(16\) Evidence of bonding capacity, in the form of a performance bond or irrevocable letter of credit in the amount required by Section 9-5-17 of this Article, or a commitment letter from a surety company or financial institution authorized to do business in the State of Illinois demonstrating the applicant\'s ability to obtain the required bond or letter of credit prior to permit issuance;
>
> \(17\) A signed background check authorization form for each principal officer, as required by Section 9-5-22 of this Article;
>
> \(18\) A non-refundable application fee of five thousand dollars (\$5,000.00) as set forth in Section 9-5-6 of this Article; and
>
> \(19\) Any additional information, documentation, or materials as may be reasonably requested by the Cannabis Licensing Coordinator to evaluate the application.

**(C)** All application materials shall be submitted in both hard copy (three complete sets) and electronic format (PDF or other format specified by the Cannabis Licensing Coordinator). The applicant is responsible for ensuring that all materials are legible, complete, and accurate.

**(D)** The applicant shall promptly notify the Cannabis Licensing Coordinator of any material changes to the information or documentation submitted in the application, including but not limited to changes in ownership, principal officers, proposed premises, or financial condition, at any time during the pendency of the application.

**[Section 9-5-6. Application Fee]{.underline}**

**(A)** The application fee for a cannabis business establishment permit under this Article shall be five thousand dollars (\$5,000.00).

**(B)** The application fee is non-refundable regardless of whether the application is approved, denied, withdrawn, or deemed incomplete. The application fee is intended to defray the City\'s costs of processing, reviewing, and evaluating the application, including staff time, background check coordination, legal review, and public hearing administration.

**(C)** Payment of the application fee shall be made by certified check or electronic funds transfer payable to the City of Evanston. Personal checks, cash, and credit card payments shall not be accepted for application fees.

**(D)** No application shall be deemed complete or accepted for review until the application fee has been received and verified by the Cannabis Licensing Coordinator. An application submitted without the required fee shall be returned to the applicant without review.

**[Section 9-5-7. Application Review Process; Timeline]{.underline}**

**(A) Application Window.** Applications for cannabis business establishment permits shall be accepted only during an open application window as designated by the City. The City shall publish notice of each application window on the City of Evanston\'s official website and in a newspaper of general circulation within the City at least thirty (30) days prior to the opening of the application window. The current application window is February 1, 2024 through May 31, 2024. Applications received outside of a designated application window shall not be accepted.

**(B) Order of Review.** Applications shall be reviewed in the order in which they are received by the Cannabis Licensing Coordinator. For purposes of establishing the order of receipt, the date and time stamp affixed by the Department of Community Development upon physical receipt of the application at the Lorraine H. Morton Civic Center shall be controlling.

**(C) Completeness Determination.** Within ninety (90) calendar days of receipt of an application, the Cannabis Licensing Coordinator shall make a completeness determination. The Coordinator shall review the application to verify that all required materials, documents, and information identified in Section 9-5-5 have been provided. If the application is determined to be incomplete, the Coordinator shall issue a written notice of deficiency to the applicant, identifying all missing or insufficient items with reasonable specificity. The applicant shall have thirty (30) calendar days from the date of the notice of deficiency to cure all identified deficiencies and submit supplemental materials. If the applicant fails to cure all deficiencies within the thirty-day cure period, the application shall be deemed withdrawn, and no refund of the application fee shall be issued.

**(D) Public Hearing.** Upon determination that an application is complete, the Cannabis Licensing Coordinator shall schedule a public hearing before the Evanston Liquor & Cannabis Board. The public hearing shall be held within forty-five (45) calendar days of the completeness determination. Notice of the public hearing shall be published on the City\'s website and mailed to all property owners within five hundred (500) feet of the proposed premises at least fifteen (15) days prior to the hearing. At the public hearing, the applicant shall have the opportunity to present its application, and members of the public shall have the opportunity to provide testimony and comments in support of or in opposition to the application.

**(E) Board Recommendation.** Following the close of the public hearing, the Liquor & Cannabis Board shall deliberate and issue a written recommendation to the City Council regarding the issuance or denial of the permit. If a special use permit is required under Section 9-5-9, the Liquor & Cannabis Board shall also forward its recommendation to the Zoning Board of Appeals for consideration in the special use permit process. The Board\'s recommendation shall address the applicant\'s compliance with all requirements of this Article and shall include the application score calculated under Section 9-5-10.

**(F) City Council Decision.** The City Council shall consider the Liquor & Cannabis Board\'s recommendation at a regular or special meeting of the City Council and shall vote on the issuance or denial of the cannabis business establishment permit. The City Council may approve the permit, approve the permit with conditions, or deny the permit. The City Council\'s decision shall be final and shall be reduced to writing and provided to the applicant within ten (10) business days of the vote.

**(G) Pre-Issuance Conditions.** No permit shall be physically issued to the applicant until all pre-issuance conditions set forth in Section 9-5-11 of this Article, and any additional conditions imposed by the City Council, have been satisfied to the satisfaction of the Cannabis Licensing Coordinator.

**[Section 9-5-8. Community Engagement Requirements]{.underline}**

**(A) Minimum Sessions Required.** Prior to the submission of an application under this Article, the applicant shall hold a minimum of two (2) community engagement sessions within the corporate limits of the City of Evanston. The purpose of the community engagement sessions is to inform local residents, business owners, neighborhood organizations, and community stakeholders about the proposed cannabis business establishment, to solicit public input and feedback, and to afford the applicant an opportunity to address community questions and concerns regarding the proposed use.

**(B) Advance Notice.** Each community engagement session shall be publicly noticed a minimum of fourteen (14) calendar days in advance of the session by the following methods:

> \(1\) Publication of a notice in a newspaper of general circulation within the City of Evanston, such notice to be published not later than fourteen (14) calendar days prior to the date of the community engagement session; and
>
> \(2\) Posting of a notice on the City of Evanston\'s official website. The applicant shall coordinate with the Cannabis Licensing Coordinator to arrange for such posting not later than fourteen (14) calendar days prior to the date of the community engagement session. The applicant shall submit the notice content and all required information to the Cannabis Licensing Coordinator at least three (3) business days prior to the required posting date to allow sufficient time for processing and publication.

**(C) Notice Content.** Each notice published or posted pursuant to subsection (B) shall include the following information:

> \(1\) The applicant\'s full legal name and the name of the proposed cannabis business establishment, if different;
>
> \(2\) The proposed street address and general location of the cannabis business establishment;
>
> \(3\) The date, time, and location of the community engagement session, including the name and address of the venue;
>
> \(4\) A brief description of the proposed use, including whether the establishment will serve adult-use customers, medical patients, or both; and
>
> \(5\) Contact information for the applicant or the applicant\'s designated representative, including a telephone number and email address at which members of the public may direct questions or comments.

**(D) Session Format.** Each community engagement session shall include a presentation by the applicant describing the proposed cannabis business establishment, including its location, operations, security measures, community benefits, and other relevant information. Each session shall also include a dedicated period for public comment and questions from attendees. The applicant shall make reasonable efforts to schedule sessions at times and locations accessible to the general public, including at least one session held during evening hours after 5:00 PM.

**(E) Documentation.** The applicant shall submit, as part of its application under Section 9-5-5, the following documentation for each community engagement session:

> \(1\) Copies of all published notices, including the date of publication, the name of the newspaper in which the notice was published, and proof of publication (such as a publisher\'s affidavit or a copy of the published notice);
>
> \(2\) Evidence of posting on the City of Evanston\'s official website, including the date of posting;
>
> \(3\) A sign-in sheet or attendance record for each session, documenting the names and, if voluntarily provided, addresses of attendees;
>
> \(4\) A written summary of each session, including the date, time, location, and duration of the session, the topics discussed, the questions raised by attendees, the concerns expressed by attendees, and the applicant\'s responses to such questions and concerns; and
>
> \(5\) Any written comments or correspondence received by the applicant from members of the public in connection with the community engagement sessions.

**(F) Consequence of Non-Compliance.** Failure to comply with the notice requirements, minimum session requirements, or documentation requirements of this Section shall render the application incomplete. The Cannabis Licensing Coordinator shall issue a written notice of deficiency pursuant to Section 9-5-7(C), and the applicant shall be required to hold additional community engagement sessions in full compliance with this Section before the application may proceed. No waiver of the minimum two-session requirement shall be granted.

**[Section 9-5-9. Location Requirements; Buffer Zones; Zoning]{.underline}**

**(A) Permitted Zoning Districts.** Cannabis dispensaries shall be permitted only within the following zoning districts, and only upon the granting of a special use permit by the Zoning Board of Appeals:

> \(1\) C1a Commercial Mixed-Use District;
>
> \(2\) C2 Commercial-Service District; and
>
> \(3\) oC1 Office-Commercial District.

No cannabis dispensary shall be located in any zoning district not listed in this subsection. A special use permit issued by the Zoning Board of Appeals is required in each such district prior to the commencement of operations.

**(B) Buffer Distances.** No cannabis dispensary shall be located within:

> \(1\) One thousand (1,000) feet of any K-12 school, as defined in Section 9-5-2;
>
> \(2\) Five hundred (500) feet of any daycare center, as defined in Section 9-5-2; or
>
> \(3\) One thousand five hundred (1,500) feet of any other licensed cannabis dispensary operating within the City of Evanston.

**(C) Measurement of Buffer Distances.** The buffer distances set forth in subsection (B) shall be measured from the nearest property line of the proposed cannabis dispensary premises to the nearest property line of the protected use.

**(D) Special Use Approval Process.** The special use approval process for cannabis dispensaries shall be conducted as follows:

> \(1\) The applicant shall submit a special use permit application to the Zoning Board of Appeals, together with a site plan, floor plan, and all other documentation required by the Zoning Board of Appeals for special use applications;
>
> \(2\) Written notice of the special use permit hearing shall be mailed by the applicant, via first-class United States mail, to all owners of property located within five hundred (500) feet of the proposed premises, as shown on the most recent records of the Cook County Assessor\'s Office, at least fifteen (15) days prior to the date of the public hearing;
>
> \(3\) The Zoning Board of Appeals shall conduct a public hearing on the special use permit application at which the applicant and members of the public may present testimony and evidence;
>
> \(4\) Following the close of the public hearing, the Zoning Board of Appeals shall issue a written recommendation to the City Council regarding the approval or denial of the special use permit, which recommendation shall address the applicable standards for special use permits under the Evanston Zoning Ordinance; and
>
> \(5\) The City Council shall consider the Zoning Board of Appeals\' recommendation and shall approve or deny the special use permit by majority vote. The City Council\'s decision shall be final.

**(E) Uses Not Subject to Buffer Requirements.** The following uses are expressly excluded from the buffer zone requirements of this Section and shall not be considered protected uses for purposes of measuring buffer distances under subsection (B): religious institutions (including churches, synagogues, mosques, temples, and other houses of worship), public parks, public libraries, hospitals, and residential properties. The proximity of the proposed cannabis dispensary to any such excluded use shall not constitute a basis for denial of a permit under this Article.

**(F) One Dispensary Per Block.** No more than one (1) cannabis dispensary shall be permitted per City block. For purposes of this subsection, \"City block\" means the area bounded by the nearest intersecting public streets on all sides.

**[Section 9-5-10. Application Scoring; Evaluation Criteria]{.underline}**

**(A) Scoring System.** The Cannabis Licensing Coordinator, in consultation with the Liquor & Cannabis Board, shall evaluate each complete application according to the scoring rubric set forth in this Section. The evaluation shall be based on the materials, documents, and information submitted with the application and the applicant\'s presentation at the public hearing.

**(B) Scoring Rubric.** Applications shall be scored according to the following categories and maximum point values:

  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Category**                                              **Maximum Points**
  --------------------------------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Business Plan                                             60

  Security Plan                                             50

  Community Benefits Plan                                   40

  Financial Viability                                       30

  Diversity & Inclusion Plan                                25

  Environmental Sustainability Plan                         20

  Experience & Qualifications                               25

  **Base Total**                                            **250**

  Social Equity Bonus (if applicable, see Section 9-5-19)   20

  **Maximum Total**                                         **270**
  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**(C) Category Descriptions.** Each scoring category shall be evaluated as follows:

> \(1\) **Business Plan (60 points):** Evaluation of the applicant\'s proposed business model, including market analysis, competitive positioning, financial projections for a minimum of three years, management structure, organizational chart, operational plan, customer service standards, product selection and sourcing strategy, and the applicant\'s overall readiness to commence and sustain operations. Higher scores shall be awarded to applicants that demonstrate a thorough understanding of the local market, a realistic and well-supported financial model, and a clear plan for long-term operational sustainability.
>
> \(2\) **Security Plan (50 points):** Evaluation of the proposed security measures, including the video surveillance system, alarm systems, access controls, secure vestibule design, inventory tracking systems, cash handling procedures, employee screening protocols, emergency response procedures, and compliance with Section 9-5-13 of this Article. Higher scores shall be awarded to applicants that exceed the minimum security requirements of this Article and demonstrate a proactive approach to loss prevention and public safety.
>
> \(3\) **Community Benefits Plan (40 points):** Evaluation of the applicant\'s proposed community benefit contributions, including the annual community benefit contribution under Section 9-5-15, local hiring commitments, community partnerships with local organizations and nonprofits, educational initiatives, youth prevention programs, and responsiveness to concerns raised during community engagement sessions. Higher scores shall be awarded to applicants that propose community benefit contributions exceeding the minimum required by this Article and that demonstrate genuine engagement with and responsiveness to the concerns of the surrounding community.
>
> \(4\) **Financial Viability (30 points):** Evaluation of the applicant\'s capitalization, access to capital, documented sources of funding, financial projections, evidence of secured bonding capacity, and the applicant\'s ability to meet the ongoing financial obligations of this Article, including permit fees, community benefit contributions, insurance requirements, and bonding requirements. Higher scores shall be awarded to applicants that demonstrate strong capitalization and diverse, verified sources of funding.
>
> \(5\) **Diversity & Inclusion Plan (25 points):** Evaluation of the applicant\'s plan to promote diversity in ownership, management, employment, and contracting, as set forth in Section 9-5-20 of this Article. Higher scores shall be awarded to applicants that present specific, measurable, and achievable diversity goals, established partnerships with workforce development organizations, and concrete plans for vendor and contractor diversity.
>
> \(6\) **Environmental Sustainability Plan (20 points):** Evaluation of the applicant\'s plan for energy efficiency, waste reduction, water conservation, cannabis waste disposal, and odor mitigation. Higher scores shall be awarded to applicants that propose environmentally responsible operational practices that exceed the minimum requirements of this Article and applicable State regulations.
>
> \(7\) **Experience & Qualifications (25 points):** Evaluation of the relevant experience, professional qualifications, certifications, and industry knowledge of the applicant\'s principal officers and key employees. Higher scores shall be awarded to applicants whose leadership team demonstrates significant prior experience in cannabis industry operations, regulatory compliance, retail management, or other directly relevant fields.

**(D) Tiebreaker.** In the event that two or more applicants receive the same total score, preference shall be given in the following order:

> \(1\) The applicant that qualifies as a Social Equity Applicant under Section 9-5-19;
>
> \(2\) The applicant with a higher Community Benefits Plan score;
>
> \(3\) The applicant whose proposed dispensary location is situated in a commercially underserved area of the City, as determined by the Cannabis Licensing Coordinator based on available data regarding commercial vacancy rates and retail density.

**(E)** The scoring results and the basis for the Coordinator\'s evaluation in each category shall be documented in writing and made available to the applicant upon request following the City Council\'s final decision.

**[Section 9-5-11. Permit Issuance; Conditions Precedent]{.underline}**

No cannabis business establishment permit shall be physically issued to an applicant until all of the following conditions precedent have been fully satisfied:

**(A)** The City Council has voted to approve the issuance of the permit to the applicant following completion of the application review process set forth in Section 9-5-7 of this Article;

**(B)** The applicant has paid the annual permit fee of fifteen thousand dollars (\$15,000.00) as set forth in Section 9-5-14 of this Article;

**(C)** The applicant has submitted proof of insurance coverage meeting all requirements of Section 9-5-16 of this Article, including certificates of insurance naming the City of Evanston as an additional insured on all general liability and product liability policies;

**(D)** The applicant has submitted a performance bond or irrevocable letter of credit in the amount of fifty thousand dollars (\$50,000.00) as required by Section 9-5-17 of this Article, in a form acceptable to the City Attorney;

**(E)** The applicant has completed all building code, fire code, and life safety inspections required by the City of Evanston and has received a Certificate of Occupancy from the City\'s Building Division for the proposed premises in its intended configuration as a cannabis dispensary;

**(F)** All security systems required by Section 9-5-13 of this Article have been installed, tested, and verified as operational by an inspection conducted by the Cannabis Licensing Coordinator or the Evanston Police Department;

**(G)** The applicant has provided verification that all employees who will be working at the dispensary upon opening have been issued valid Illinois Cannabis Dispensing Organization Agent Identification Cards by the Illinois Department of Financial and Professional Regulation;

**(H)** The applicant has executed and submitted a signed community benefit agreement in accordance with Section 9-5-15 of this Article, committing to the community benefit contributions and community investment obligations described in the applicant\'s approved community benefits plan;

**(I)** The applicant has provided verification that it holds a valid State of Illinois cannabis dispensing organization license, whether conditional or permanent, authorizing operations at the proposed location;

**(J)** The applicant has satisfied such other conditions as the City Council may have imposed as part of its approval of the application, including but not limited to any conditions imposed as part of a special use permit approval under Section 9-5-9.

**[Section 9-5-12. Permit Duration; Renewal]{.underline}**

**(A) Permit Term.** Each cannabis dispensary permit issued under this Article shall be valid for a period of twelve (12) months from the date of issuance, unless sooner suspended or revoked in accordance with Section 9-5-25 of this Article. A permit that is not renewed prior to its expiration shall lapse automatically on the date of expiration, and the permit holder shall immediately cease all cannabis operations at the permitted location.

**(B) Renewal Application Deadline.** A permit holder seeking renewal of a cannabis dispensary permit shall submit a complete renewal application to the Cannabis Licensing Coordinator not less than sixty (60) days and not more than ninety (90) days prior to the expiration date of the current permit. Failure to submit a timely renewal application may result in the lapse of the permit upon expiration.

**(C) Renewal Application Contents.** Each renewal application shall include the following:

> \(1\) Updated certificates of insurance demonstrating continued compliance with all insurance requirements of Section 9-5-16;
>
> \(2\) Proof of continued bonding in accordance with Section 9-5-17, including a current copy of the performance bond or irrevocable letter of credit;
>
> \(3\) Evidence of compliance with all community benefit obligations under Section 9-5-15, including payment of the most recent annual community benefit contribution and the audited statement of gross annual revenue;
>
> \(4\) Evidence of compliance with the diversity and inclusion plan required by Section 9-5-20, including current workforce demographic data documenting the residency and demographic composition of all employees;
>
> \(5\) The results of the most recent quarterly compliance self-audit conducted under Section 9-5-13(K), together with documentation of any corrective actions taken to address identified deficiencies;
>
> \(6\) Evidence of continued participation in the Cannabis Business Mentorship Program, if applicable under Section 9-5-19(E), including attendance records, workshop completion certificates, and semi-annual progress reports;
>
> \(7\) A disclosure of any changes to ownership, management, or principal officers that have occurred since the prior application or most recent renewal, together with updated background check authorizations for any new principal officers; and
>
> \(8\) Payment of the annual permit renewal fee of fifteen thousand dollars (\$15,000.00), as set forth in Section 9-5-14.

**(D) Social Equity Re-Verification.** Social equity status shall be re-verified at each annual renewal. A permit holder that received a social equity scoring bonus under Section 9-5-19 shall demonstrate continued compliance with all social equity conditions at each renewal, including but not limited to the workforce hiring requirements of Section 9-5-19(D) and the mentorship program participation requirements of Section 9-5-19(E). The Cannabis Licensing Coordinator shall review the renewal application and all supporting documentation to determine whether the permit holder remains in compliance with all social equity conditions. Failure to maintain compliance with social equity conditions may result in revocation of social equity status for the current and all future renewal periods and may constitute grounds for permit revocation under Section 9-5-25.

**(E) Renewal Decision.** The Cannabis Licensing Coordinator shall review the renewal application and make a recommendation to the Liquor & Cannabis Board, which shall in turn make a recommendation to the City Council. The City Council shall approve or deny the renewal. Grounds for denial of a renewal include failure to meet any of the requirements of this Section, a pattern of violations during the preceding permit term, or the existence of grounds for revocation under Section 9-5-25.

**[Section 9-5-13. Security and Operational Requirements]{.underline}**

Every cannabis dispensary operating under a permit issued pursuant to this Article shall comply with the following security and operational requirements at all times during the term of the permit:

**(A) Video Surveillance.** The premises shall be equipped with a twenty-four (24) hour, seven (7) day-a-week digital video surveillance system. All cameras shall record at a minimum resolution of 1080p (1920 × 1080 pixels) and shall provide clear and identifiable images of all persons and activities in the recorded areas. Recordings shall be stored on a secure, dedicated digital recording device located on the premises and shall be retained for a minimum of thirty (30) calendar days. Recordings shall be made available to the Evanston Police Department or the Cannabis Licensing Coordinator upon request within twenty-four (24) hours of the request. Camera coverage shall include, at a minimum, all of the following areas: all entrances and exits to the building, the retail floor area, all points of sale, all secure storage areas, all areas where cannabis products are handled or processed, the exterior of the premises including parking areas and loading zones, and any alleyways immediately adjacent to the premises. The surveillance system shall be maintained in good working order at all times, and the permit holder shall promptly repair or replace any camera or recording device that is malfunctioning or inoperative.

**(B) Panic Alarm.** The premises shall be equipped with a panic alarm system connected directly to the Evanston Police Department or to a licensed third-party monitoring service that is capable of immediately dispatching the Evanston Police Department in the event of an activation. Panic alarm activation points shall be located at each point of sale, at the main entrance, and in the secure storage area. The panic alarm system shall be tested no less than once per calendar quarter, and written documentation of each test, including the date, the method of testing, and the results, shall be maintained on-site and made available for inspection upon request.

**(C) Secure Vestibule Entry.** The dispensary shall include a secure vestibule or controlled entry area through which all customers must pass before being admitted to the retail floor area. The vestibule shall be designed to permit the dispensary to control and restrict access to the retail floor area. No customer shall be admitted to the retail floor area until the customer\'s age and identity have been verified by a dispensary agent through examination of a valid, unexpired government-issued photo identification document. Acceptable forms of identification include a state driver\'s license, state identification card, United States passport, or United States military identification card.

**(D) Seed-to-Sale Tracking.** The dispensary shall utilize a seed-to-sale tracking system that integrates with the State of Illinois COMPASS system (Cannabis Operations and Management Platform for Administrative and Scientific Services) or any successor system designated by the State. All inventory movements, including receipts, sales, returns, transfers, disposals, and any other changes in inventory status, shall be recorded in the seed-to-sale tracking system in real time. The permit holder shall ensure that the tracking system is accurate, up to date, and reconciled with physical inventory on a daily basis.

**(E) Employee Identification.** All employees working in the dispensary, including the Agent-in-Charge and all other agents, shall hold a valid, current Illinois Cannabis Dispensing Organization Agent Identification Card issued by the Illinois Department of Financial and Professional Regulation. No person shall be permitted to begin employment or perform any duties at the dispensary until such identification card has been issued by the State. Agent identification cards shall be worn or displayed by employees at all times while on the premises during hours of operation.

**(F) Operating Hours.** Cannabis dispensaries shall be open for business to the public only between the hours of 6:00 AM and 10:00 PM daily, including Saturdays, Sundays, and holidays. No sales or dispensing of cannabis products shall occur outside of these hours. Employees may be present on the premises outside of operating hours for purposes of inventory management, cleaning, security, and administrative tasks, provided that no cannabis products are sold or dispensed during non-operating hours.

**(G) Odor Mitigation.** The dispensary shall implement and continuously maintain an odor mitigation plan designed to prevent cannabis-related odors from migrating beyond the property line of the premises. The odor mitigation plan shall include, at a minimum, HVAC systems equipped with activated carbon filtration units sufficient to neutralize cannabis odors, negative air pressure within the dispensary relative to the outside environment, and sealed entry and exit points. The odor mitigation system shall be certified by a licensed professional engineer registered in the State of Illinois prior to permit issuance and shall be inspected and recertified annually at the time of permit renewal. The permit holder shall promptly address any odor complaints received from neighboring property owners or the City.

**(H) Waste Disposal.** All cannabis waste, including unusable cannabis product, expired products, contaminated materials, and packaging waste, shall be disposed of in accordance with the regulations of the Illinois Environmental Protection Agency and the Illinois Cannabis Regulation and Tax Act. The dispensary shall maintain a written waste disposal plan, which shall be made available for inspection upon request. Records of all waste disposals, including the date of disposal, the type and quantity of waste, the method of disposal, and the identity of the disposal service provider, shall be maintained for a minimum of five (5) years.

**(I) Agent-in-Charge.** Each dispensary shall designate an Agent-in-Charge who shall be responsible for ensuring compliance with this Article and all applicable State law during all hours of operation. The Agent-in-Charge shall be present on the premises during all hours of operation or, in the event of an emergency or unforeseen absence, available to return to the premises within thirty (30) minutes. The identity and contact information of the Agent-in-Charge shall be provided to the Cannabis Licensing Coordinator and updated within five (5) business days of any change.

**(J) Maximum Occupancy.** The maximum number of customers permitted in the retail floor area at any one time shall be calculated at a rate of one (1) customer per forty (40) square feet of retail floor area, as that term is defined in Section 9-5-2 of this Article. For example, a dispensary with one thousand six hundred (1,600) square feet of retail floor area shall have a maximum customer occupancy of forty (40) customers in the retail floor area at any one time. The permit holder shall implement procedures to monitor and enforce the maximum occupancy limit, including a queuing system in the vestibule or waiting area if necessary. The maximum customer occupancy shall be posted conspicuously at the entrance to the retail floor area.

**(K) Quarterly Compliance Self-Audits.** Every permit holder shall conduct a comprehensive compliance self-audit at the end of each calendar quarter, as defined in Section 9-5-2 of this Article. The self-audit shall review the permit holder\'s compliance with all provisions of this Article, all conditions of the permit, all applicable provisions of the Illinois Cannabis Regulation and Tax Act and the implementing regulations, and any additional conditions imposed by the City Council. The self-audit shall include, at a minimum, a review of security system functionality, inventory accuracy, employee licensing status, insurance and bonding compliance, record-keeping practices, odor mitigation system performance, and community benefit obligations.

The results of each quarterly self-audit, including a description of all areas reviewed, all identified deficiencies, the corrective actions taken or planned to address each deficiency, and the timeline for completion of corrective actions, shall be submitted in writing to the Cannabis Licensing Coordinator within fifteen (15) calendar days following the end of the applicable calendar quarter. For purposes of this subsection, the applicable calendar quarters and their corresponding submission deadlines are as follows:

> Quarter 1 (January 1 through March 31): Self-audit report due by April 15;
>
> Quarter 2 (April 1 through June 30): Self-audit report due by July 15;
>
> Quarter 3 (July 1 through September 30): Self-audit report due by October 15;
>
> Quarter 4 (October 1 through December 31): Self-audit report due by January 15 of the following year.

Failure to conduct a quarterly self-audit or to submit the self-audit report by the applicable deadline shall constitute a violation of this Article.

**[Section 9-5-14. Annual Permit Fee]{.underline}**

**(A)** The annual cannabis dispensary permit fee shall be fifteen thousand dollars (\$15,000.00), payable prior to the issuance of the initial permit and upon each annual renewal as a condition of renewal.

**(B)** The fee shall be payable by certified check or electronic funds transfer to the City of Evanston. Personal checks, cash, and credit card payments shall not be accepted for permit fees.

**(C)** Permit fees are non-refundable, including in the event of permit suspension, revocation, or voluntary surrender.

**(D)** The City Council reserves the right to adjust the annual permit fee by resolution at any time. Any adjustment to the fee shall take effect at the beginning of the next permit year following the adoption of the resolution.

**[Section 9-5-15. Community Benefit Contribution]{.underline}**

**(A) Requirement.** Every cannabis dispensary permit holder operating under a permit issued pursuant to this Article shall make an annual community benefit contribution to the City of Evanston Cannabis Community Benefit Fund.

**(B) Amount.** The annual community benefit contribution shall be the greater of:

> \(1\) Twenty-five thousand dollars (\$25,000.00); or
>
> \(2\) One percent (1%) of the permit holder\'s gross annual revenue derived from cannabis sales at the permitted location during the applicable permit year.

**(C) Due Date.** The community benefit contribution shall be due and payable within ninety (90) calendar days following the end of each permit year. The permit year shall be calculated from the date of initial permit issuance.

**(D) Audited Revenue Statement.** At the time the community benefit contribution is paid, the permit holder shall submit to the Cannabis Licensing Coordinator an audited statement of gross annual revenue for the applicable permit year, prepared by a certified public accountant licensed in the State of Illinois. The audited statement shall detail total gross revenue from cannabis sales, including a breakdown of adult-use and medical sales, and shall be prepared in accordance with generally accepted accounting principles.

**(E) Use of Funds.** The Cannabis Community Benefit Fund shall be administered by the Department of Community Development and shall be used exclusively for the following purposes:

> \(1\) Workforce development programs in R3-designated census tracts within the City of Evanston;
>
> \(2\) Community health and wellness initiatives, including substance abuse prevention and treatment programs;
>
> \(3\) Youth education and cannabis prevention programs in Evanston public and private schools;
>
> \(4\) Restorative justice programs, including expungement clinics and re-entry services;
>
> \(5\) Infrastructure improvements, including streetscaping, lighting, and public amenities, in R3-designated census tracts; and
>
> \(6\) Small business development grants for businesses located in R3-designated census tracts.

**(F) Enforcement.** Failure to pay the community benefit contribution when due, or failure to submit the required audited revenue statement, shall constitute a violation of this Article and grounds for permit suspension or revocation under Section 9-5-25.

**[Section 9-5-16. Insurance Requirements]{.underline}**

**(A) Required Coverages.** Prior to the issuance of a cannabis dispensary permit, and maintained continuously throughout the term of the permit and any renewal thereof, the permit holder shall maintain the following insurance coverages with insurers authorized to do business in the State of Illinois:

> \(1\) **Commercial General Liability Insurance** with limits of not less than two million dollars (\$2,000,000.00) per occurrence and four million dollars (\$4,000,000.00) in the annual aggregate, covering bodily injury, property damage, personal injury, and advertising injury arising out of the permit holder\'s operations;
>
> \(2\) **Product Liability Insurance** with limits of not less than two million dollars (\$2,000,000.00) per occurrence and four million dollars (\$4,000,000.00) in the annual aggregate, covering claims arising from the sale, distribution, or dispensing of cannabis and cannabis-infused products;
>
> \(3\) **Workers\' Compensation Insurance** at the statutory limits required by the Illinois Workers\' Compensation Act (820 ILCS 305/) and Employer\'s Liability coverage with limits of not less than one million dollars (\$1,000,000.00) per accident; and
>
> \(4\) **Cannabis-Specific Crime and Theft Insurance** with limits of not less than five hundred thousand dollars (\$500,000.00), covering losses resulting from theft, employee dishonesty, robbery, burglary, and mysterious disappearance of cannabis inventory.
